headline: Paris Harnesses the Seine to Cool Buildings Naturally
meta_description: Paris is using the Seine river to cool thousands of buildings without air conditioning, reducing urban heat and energy waste.
summary_short: Paris has engineered an ingenious system that uses the Seine river to naturally cool thousands of buildings, cutting energy use and heat pollution.
body:
As cities grow hotter, most reach for the same solution: air conditioning units pulling heat from indoors and dumping it outside. When millions of units operate together on a sweltering day, they paradoxically make streets hotter, intensifying the heat that drives the next crisis. Paris found another way.

The city has tapped the Seine itself as a natural cooling system. Rather than forcing buildings to generate and expel their own waste heat, the river's water circulates through a network, absorbing excess warmth and carrying it away. Thousands of buildings now benefit from this approach.

The system is elegant because it solves multiple problems at once. It reduces energy consumption—meaning less electricity demand and fewer emissions from power plants. It lowers street temperatures, making neighbourhoods more liveable during heat waves. And it works with the city's natural geography instead of against it, turning a river that runs through Paris into an infrastructure asset. It's a reminder that sometimes the best solutions aren't the newest technologies, but the wisdom to work with what's already there.
